explanation_md: The array has 4 elements but only 3 are listed in the initializer. The unlisted element at index 3 is value-initialized to 0. So `a[0]` is 1 and `a[3]` is 0, printing `1 0`.

prompt_md: Which declaration is valid for a `std::array` of 5 integers?

options.c: `int n = 5; std::array<int, n> arr {};` — valid because n holds 5

explanation_md: Option b is correct: `std::array<int, 5> arr {}` value-initializes all 5 elements to 0. Option a leaves elements uninitialized on the stack. Option c uses a runtime variable as the size template argument, which the compiler rejects. Option d has incorrect syntax.
The range-based for loop visits each element in order. Each is multiplied by 2 and printed with a space: 20, 40, 60. The trailing newline ends the line but produces no extra visible output.

prompt_md: What is the key difference between accessing an element with `arr[i]` versus `arr.at(i)`?

options.a: `arr[i]` is slower because it checks bounds; `arr.at(i)` skips bounds checking
options.b: `arr.at(i)` performs a bounds check at runtime; `arr[i]` does not
options.c: Both perform bounds checks, but `arr.at(i)` also checks at compile time
options.d: `arr[i]` can only be used in a for loop; `arr.at(i)` works anywhere

explanation_md: `arr.at(i)` checks whether `i` is a valid index at runtime and throws `std::out_of_range` if not. `arr[i]` has no bounds check — an out-of-range index causes undefined behavior silently. There is no compile-time bounds check for either.
